title: Vladimir Grig Thank You for Being With Us Dreams
description: Multimedia Art Museum, Moscow | Exhibitions | Vladimir Grig - Thank You for Being With Us Dreams Ru Buy a ticket Museum Exhibitions biennales Books Rodchenko school Vladimir Grig Thank You for Being With Us Dreams 1 2 3 4 Moscow, 27.09.2025—15.02.2026 exhibition is over Share with friends Curators: Anna Zaitseva, Maria Lavrova Co-organizer: Gridchinhall Gallery Curators: Anna Zaitseva, Maria Lavrova Co-organizer: Gridchinhall Gallery Close For the press About the exhibition Vladimir Grig Thank You for Being With Us Dreams Curators : Anna Zaitseva, Maria Lavrova Co-organiser: Gridchinhall Gallery The Multimedia Art Museum, Moscow presents a solo exhibition by Vladimir Grig, one of the most prominent contemporary artists, as well as a sculptor and musician. Grig is a nominee for the Kandinsky and Kuryokhin Prizes, the recipient of a Berlin Academy of Arts grant, and a participant in the parallel programme of the 55th Venice Biennale. His works feature in the collections of Russian museums including the State Russian Museum, and in numerous private collections. Vladimir Grig was born in Krasnodar in 1962. After graduating from Krasnodar Art College he worked for several years as a book illustrator for major publishing houses, which significantly influenced his visual language and instilled in him a love for and attention to textual sources. “At some point I became aware of my ‘literary disposition’ and accepted it as something very organic for me,” explains the artist. Most of Grig’s works presented in the exhibition are executed in the complex and labour-intensive technique of glass bead mosaic on canvas that he has practised in recent years. Mosaics, which originated in the second half of the 4th millennium BC and were widely used in the architecture of ancient Greece and Rome, as well as for the design of Soviet public institutions, became the ideal medium for Grig’s postmodern artistic practice. Using a variety of media, Vladimir Grig creates fantastical, sometimes hallucinogenic worlds filled with visual alogisms. Akin to medieval murals or book miniatures, his works are hardly subject to the laws of lifelike portrayal. In constant dialogue with his predecessors and contemporaries, from Pieter Bruegel and Ivan Bilibin to Viktor Pivovarov and Takashi Murakami, Grig ironically and freely plays with art history and popular culture. For example, in the ‘Absolute Happiness of the 1960s’ series featured at the exhibition, the traditions of Soviet book illustration, 1950s-1960s print design, and science fiction are combined with the fairy-tale, mystical atmosphere of Bilibin and Vasnetsov’s works, evoking the aesthetics of Russian Art Nouveau. The characters in Grig’s multiverse transcend the boundaries of space and time, travelling through styles, eras and cultural layers while assuming new and unexpected roles. description: Multimedia Art Museum, Moscow | Exhibitions | Dmitry Bulin - Between realms Ru Buy a ticket Museum Exhibitions biennales Books Rodchenko school Dmitry Bulin Between realms 1 2 3 4 5 Dmitry Bulin. Courtesy of the author. Dmitry Bulin. Vow. 2025. Digital printing. Courtesy of the author. Dmitry Bulin. Choosing the Bride. 2025. Digital printing. Courtesy of the author. Moscow, 4.07.2025—14.09.2025 exhibition is over Share with friends Curators: Anna Zaitseva, Maria Lavrova Curators: Anna Zaitseva, Maria Lavrova Close For the press About the exhibition DMITRY BULIN Between Realms Curators: Anna Zaitseva, Maria Lavrova As part of the biennale Fashion and Style in Photography – 2025, the Multimedia Art Museum, Moscow presents an exhibition by contemporary photographer Dmitry Bulin. In his new project ‘Between Realms’ (2025), created specifically for the MAMM exhibition, Dmitry Bulin turns to Russian folklore and ritual motifs. Today the Russian folk tale is the leading trend of modern Russian culture. It serves as an inexhaustible resource for artists, producers, directors and fashion designers. The increased demand for folk tales makes them more than merely an object of study and interpretation; they are also frequently simplified as entertainment. Being a sensitive and subtle artist, Bulin is distinguished by his thoughtful, careful approach to the material, his understanding of its multi-layered character and depth. In studying Russian folk tales, epics, legends and other genres of folklore, Bulin plastically rethinks their cross-cutting themes and images. In particular he focuses on the key fairy tale motifs of duality, dual worlds, transcendental transition and metamorphosis. Bulin’s heroes journey between two worlds: the earthly and otherworldly, aquatic and underwater, accompanied by the birds Sirin or Alkonost, and other mythical creatures. While working on the project the photographer referred to the research of famous philologist and folklorist Vladimir Propp. In his programmatic work ‘Morphology of the Folk Tale’ (1928) Propp studied how a fairy tale is structured. He analysed one hundred works from the most complete collection, ‘Russian Folk Tales’, compiled by the ethnographer and folklore collector Alexander Afanasyev, and found that they all have a universal narrative structure. Hence the folk-tale character has seven main roles (the hero, villain, donor, helper, dispatcher, princess (or her father) and false hero) and 31 functions (prohibition – violation, persecution – salvation, etc.). Propp’s findings are still used by writers, screenwriters and video game developers all over the world. title: Valery Plotnikov Heroes of their time
description: Multimedia Art Museum, Moscow | Exhibitions | Valery Plotnikov - Heroes of their time Ru Buy a ticket Museum Exhibitions biennales Books Rodchenko school Valery Plotnikov Heroes of their time 1 2 3 4 5 6 7 8 9 Khabarovsk, 19.06—16.08.2026 39 days left The Far Eastern Art Museum Share with friends Curator: Olga Sviblova Curator: Olga Sviblova Close Exhibition shedule 4.07.2025—14.09.2025 Moscow Multimedia Art Museum 5.03—19.04.2026 Nizhniy Novgorod Russian Museum of Photography 19.06—16.08.2026 Khabarovsk The Far Eastern Art Museum For the press About the exhibition VALERY PLOTNIKOV Heroes of Their Time Curator: Olga Sviblova Project realised on the initiative and with the support of the Still Art Foundation As part of the biennale Fashion and Style in Photography – 2025, the Multimedia Art Museum, Moscow presents the exhibition ‘Heroes of Their Time’ by classic portrait photographer Valery Plotnikov, the author of hundreds of portraits featuring prominent figures in Soviet and Russian art, including Vladimir Vysotsky, Joseph Brodsky, Mikhail Baryshnikov, Svyatoslav Richter, Oleg Yankovsky, Vyacheslav Zaitsev, and others. Legendary film director Grigory Kozintsev aptly defined Plotnikov as a photographer-director who “conceives photography as a production”. Each of his shots is like a film or performance compressed into one frame. Every detail of the artistic space is thoroughly devised and meaningful. When meeting the subject Plotnikov creates an individual environment for them, selecting the right background, clothes, hairstyle, pose and props, with nothing left to chance. “A well-found image allows you to find the right sense of self,” the photographer believes. Valery Plotnikov was born in 1943 in Barnaul, during evacuation. In 1945 his family returned to Leningrad. Plotnikov graduated from the Leningrad Secondary Art School at the Academy of Arts and in 1964 began studying at the VGIK cinematography department, where he became keen on photography. His first subjects were college friends, Sergei Solovyov and Lev Dodin, later to become famous directors, as well as the playwright Eduard Volodarsky. After completing his course at the institute Plotnikov took up photography professionally. The photographer recalls that even in his youth he “knew how to guess the significance of a person”. This talent helped realise his ambitious dream: to preserve the faces of heroes of their time for future generations. Plotnikov shot portraits of both rising stars and those already “in their declining years”. He was interested in representatives of bohemia and the creative intelligentsia across the broadest possible spectrum: from the public’s favourite actors Andrei Mironov and Mikhail Boyarsky to academician Dmitry Likhachev and the great pianist Mstislav Rostropovich. title: Dmitry Bulin We Dream This
description: Multimedia Art Museum, Moscow | Exhibitions | Dmitry Bulin - We Dream This Ru Buy a ticket Museum Exhibitions biennales Books Rodchenko school Dmitry Bulin We Dream This 1 2 3 4 5 Dmitry Bulin Mirror of Venus. 2024. Digital printing. Project realised for The Blueprint publication. Dmitry Bulin. Id. 2024. Digital printing. Project realised for The Blueprint publication. Moscow, 4.07.2025—14.09.2025 exhibition is over Share with friends Curators: Anna Zaitseva, Maria Lavrova Curators: Anna Zaitseva, Maria Lavrova Close For the press About the exhibition DMITRY BULIN We Dream This Curators: Anna Zaitseva, Maria Lavrova As part of the biennale Fashion and Style in Photography – 2025, the Multimedia Art Museum, Moscow presents the exhibition ‘We Dream This’ by the contemporary photographer Dmitry Bulin. In 2022 MAMM hosted the first solo exhibition project by Bulin, who won the main prize of the Still Art Foundation grant programme in the field of fashion photography. This new exhibition showcases images where he enters into a fascinating dialogue with surrealism, demonstrating the development of his artistic language. Dmitry Bulin’s creative method is based on constant interaction with works of world culture and art, their new and up-to-date interpretation. The project ‘We Dream This’, created for The Blueprint in 2024 as part of a special issue dedicated to the centenary of surrealism (creative idea: Alexander Perepelkin, creative director and stylist: Igor Andreev), is in homage to the highly significant movement that established a new mode of artistic thinking. In 1924 the French poet and ideologist of surrealism André Breton published a manifesto in which he rejected rationalism and practical utility, calling for creative inspiration in our own subconscious, fantasies and dreams. Surrealism was greatly influenced by psychoanalysis, which was actively evolving at that time, primarily the ideas of Sigmund Freud outlined in ‘The Interpretation of Dreams’, as well as Carl Jung’s theory of the collective unconscious. According to the latter, universal images or archetypes are embedded in the psyche of every human being and made manifest in myths, fairy tales, art, religion, etc. In his pictures Bulin offers original interpretations of the iconic images of surrealism presented in the works of classic artists Salvador Dali (‘The Elephants’), René Magritte (‘L’Embellie’), Giorgio de Chirico (‘Song of Love’), and Man Ray (‘Kiki de Montparnasse’). title: Detective stories from the life of a museum curator. Episode two
description: Multimedia Art Museum, Moscow | Exhibitions | Detective stories from the life of a museum curator. Episode two Ru Buy a ticket Museum Exhibitions biennales Books Rodchenko school Detective stories from the life of a museum curator. Episode two 1 2 3 4 5 6 Moscow, 4.07.2025—7.12.2025 exhibition is over Share with friends Curators: Anna Zaitseva, Maria Lavrova Curators: Anna Zaitseva, Maria Lavrova Close For the press About the exhibition As part of the biennale Fashion and Style in Photography – 2025 Detective Stories from the Life of a Museum Curator Curators: Anna Zaitseva, Maria Lavrova Author of detective stories: Boris Misalandi Text preparation and assistance in the investigation: Nikita Malashenko MAMM continues the series of exhibitions ‘Detective Stories from the Life of a Museum Curator’, which reveal the ‘inside story’ of museum work. The first part of the project introduced viewers to a fascinating exploration of photographs from the late 19th to early 20th centuries. This time we focus on images from the 1950s to early 2000s, researched by the curator Boris Misalandi. He talks about his most complex, exciting and sometimes even unfinished ‘investigations’. The exhibition presents photographs by recognised masters of Russian photography such as Vsevolod Tarasevich, Dmitry Baltermants, Yevgeny Khaldei, Alexander Slyusarev and others, as well as little-known pictures with extremely interesting stories. A museum curator is not only charged with preservation of the objects entrusted to him. Properly speaking, the museum life of a photograph can only begin when we place it in a historical, artistic and cultural context. Curators are charged with the painstaking assignment of collecting and interpreting information, along with other tasks. Their work is a valuable contribution to the history of art, the basis for scientific research and curatorial projects. When photographs come to a museum, the locations, dates and circumstances of the shooting may be unknown, although in principal the events recorded seem self-evident. Establishing these facts is called attribution. But even when a picture arrives at the museum with a detailed description, the curator faces many tasks. Every detail needs to be double-checked, every connection documented, and additional information must be collected to help the modern viewer fully understand the history of subjects and events captured in the frame. Museum visitors usually see only the results of curatorial research: the signatures for exhibits, booklets, explanatory texts… But now, at this exhibition, Boris Misalandi reveals the secrets of the museum’s scientific life: as an experienced ‘detective’, he reconstructs the path from riddles to solutions, guiding the visitors on their way. He shows what studying photographs really entails, and how they are attributed. title: From the Beatles to Galliano. The Star Odyssey of Jean-Marie Perrier
description: Multimedia Art Museum, Moscow | Exhibitions | From the Beatles to Galliano. The Star Odyssey of Jean-Marie Perrier Ru Buy a ticket Museum Exhibitions biennales Books Rodchenko school From the Beatles to Galliano. The Star Odyssey of Jean-Marie Perrier 1 2 3 4 5 6 7 Moscow, 4.07.2025—14.09.2025 exhibition is over Share with friends Close For the press About the exhibition From the Beatles to Galliano. The Starry Odyssey of Jean-Marie Périer Curators: Anna Zaitseva, Maria Lavrova Co-organiser: Lumiere Gallery As part of the biennale Fashion and Style in Photography – 2025, the Multimedia Art Museum, Moscow presents an exhibition by the renowned French photographer and director Jean-Marie Périer, author of iconic shots of the Beatles and the Rolling Stones. He captured the most famous world luminaries of the second half of the 20th century, celebrities that defined the image of the era: Brigitte Bardot, Françoise Hardy, Alain Delon, Catherine Deneuve, Monica Bellucci, Yves Saint Laurent, John Galliano, Vivienne Westwood, and so on. Jean-Marie Périer was born in 1940 to the family of popular actor François Périer, who played in a Federico Fellini film, and actress Jacqueline Porel. In 1956 he became an assistant to the photographer and publisher Daniel Filipacchi, who worked for leading magazines such as Paris Match and Marie Claire, as well as for Jazz Magazine. At the age of 17 Périer was already photographing jazz legends Ella Fitzgerald, Miles Davis and Dizzy Gillespie for covers and editorial materials. The first stage of Périer’s creative career coincided with the ‘Thirty Glorious Years’ (1946-1975) in France – a time of powerful economic growth with a booming population, social and cultural revolution. The post-war generation challenged established norms and the stable way of life, striving for independence and freedom of expression. It was the young who set trends in fashion, cinema and music in the 1960s, creating innovative trends and genres. In 1962 the youth music magazine Salut les copains appeared in France. The publisher, Daniel Filipacchi, offered Périer a position as staff photographer and gave him unlimited creative freedom with just one condition: “Photos must not please the parents”. The magazine was a resounding success. It became a symbol of the new youth culture and played an important role in its formation. Périer recalled: “The first issue was published in a circulation of 100 thousand, and there was a real battle to get your copy at the newsstands. Six months later we were already printing in editions of a million. It was a revolution, comparable in scale to the launch of MTV in America”. During his twelve years at the magazine Périer photographed many musicians who became cultural phenomena. In the mid-1960s the music world was confronted by a ‘British invasion’. English rock groups, in particular the Beatles and their main competitors the Rolling Stones, conquered not only the national, but also international charts. Périe

title: Rino Barillari King of the paparazzi
description: Multimedia Art Museum, Moscow | Exhibitions | Rino Barillari - King of the paparazzi Ru Buy a ticket Museum Exhibitions biennales Books Rodchenko school Rino Barillari King of the paparazzi 1 2 3 4 5 6 7 8 9 Moscow, 4.07.2025—14.09.2025 exhibition is over Share with friends Curators: Olga Strada, Anna Zaitseva, Maria Lavrova Curators: Olga Strada, Anna Zaitseva, Maria Lavrova Close For the press About the exhibition RINO BARILLARI King of the Paparazzi Curators: Olga Strada, Anna Zaitseva, Maria Lavrova As part of the biennale Fashion and Style in Photography – 2025, the Multimedia Art Museum, Moscow presents the exhibition ‘King of the Paparazzi’ by classic Italian photojournalist Rino Barillari. His lens captured stars of the first magnitude such as Audrey Hepburn, Elizabeth Taylor, Brigitte Bardot, Sophia Loren, Woody Allen, Claudia Schiffer, George Clooney and Bruce Willis. Over the years Barillari himself became a celebrity, becoming the epitome of the paparazzi phenomenon. As he snapped public favourites without warning in an informal setting, he regularly got involved in scuffles: he was hospitalised 165 times and his ribs broken 12 times, with 78 cameras and 40 flashes smashed. The word ‘paparazzi’ first appeared thanks to director Federico Fellini: in his acclaimed film ‘La Dolce Vita’ (1960) one of the characters, an intrusive photojournalist, is called Paparazzo. Soon the name caught on. Symbolically, according to one version, it was Fellini himself who awarded Rino Barillari the title ‘king of the paparazzi’. Rino Barillari was born in 1945 in the southern town of Limbadi, in Calabria. At the age of 14 the boy ran away from home, clad in his father’s jacket and polished shoes with holes, small change jingling in the pocket of his grandfather’s overcoat as he set off to conquer the capital. “It was a stroke of luck that Rome has the Villa Borghese Park, where I spent the night, and the Trevi Fountain – travellers threw coins there, sometimes they missed and I picked them up. That’s how I lived,” says Barillari. One of the most famous fountains in the world gave the young Rino not only his first means of livelihood, but also a life’s work. He began helping photographers who took pictures of anyone who wanted against the backdrop of this landmark (he asked other people to step out of the frame), and soon he took up the camera himself: there were a lot of tourists, people now felt free to enjoy a peaceful life as Rome recovered after several difficult decades. Entry into World War II on the side of Germany and the years of dictatorship led Italy to poverty and ruin. In the 1950s and 1960s well-equipped film studios stood idle in a poor, but still very beautiful, warm country, and people agreed to work for pennies. The excellent conditions for inexpensive film production attracted producers, and Rome gradually became ‘Hollywood on the Tiber’. It is based on videos dedicated to key events of the war, and the fates of the photographers who captured them. Among the thousands of photojournalists from 62 countries that participated in World War II, the Soviet photographers left the most poignant evidence of those terrible events that we must remember and that must not be repeated. With a camera instead of a rifle, they endured the entire war side by side with the soldiers, performing feats every day. Photographers demonstrated miracles of heroism: even a simple turn of the focus ring in the smoke and under fire seemed impossible, both physically and morally. title: Naum Granovsky Moscow: from the first taxis to Olympic construction sites
description: Multimedia Art Museum, Moscow | Exhibitions | Naum Granovsky - Moscow: from the first taxis to Olympic construction sites Ru Buy a ticket Museum Exhibitions biennales Books Rodchenko school Naum Granovsky Moscow: from the first taxis to Olympic construction sites 1 2 3 4 5 6 Moscow, 3.04.2025—4.06.2025 exhibition is over Share with friends Curators: Anna Zaitseva, Maria Lavrova Curators: Anna Zaitseva, Maria Lavrova Close For the press About the exhibition NAUM GRANOVSKY Moscow: From the First Taxis to Olympic Construction Sites Curators: Anna Zaitseva, Maria Lavrova Selection of chronicles: Maria Lavrova, Olga Mikhalchuk Editing of chronicles: Ilya Flyarkovsky Texts for the photographs: Polina Baulina, Anastasia Kuvyrtalova, Svetlana Mezentseva, Elvira Ramazanova, Nikita Slinkin, Yulia Tikhomirova Co-organiser: Lumiere Gallery The Multimedia Art Museum, Moscow presents an exhibition by classic of Russian photography Naum Granovsky, dedicated to his 115th anniversary. Granovsky is one of the finest architectural photographers, and Moscow was always the focus of his attention. Granovsky’s photographs are unique evidence of how rapidly the architectural appearance of the capital changed from the mid-1920s to the 1980s, and with it the life of the city and the Muscovites. The exhibition also features rare newsreels of Moscow, showing life in the capital over the years: the work of market squares, cabbies and taxis, the opening of the All-Union Agricultural Exhibition (now VDNKh) and the Moscow Swimming Pool, the construction of new houses and districts, the advertising of Soviet hotels, shops, etc. Naum Granovsky was born in 1910 in the city of Alexandria. He became interested in photography in his youth and took pictures with a Kodak camera given to him by his father. In 1926 Granovsky moved to Moscow, where he got a job as laboratory assistant in the newly opened Press Cliché workshop at the TASS agency. A year later he was hired as a photojournalist. The 1920s marked a period of tectonic changes in the architectural appearance of Moscow and its way of life. In 1918 it received the status of capital city and became the centre of a cultural revolution. Utopian ideas of the new government about the ‘reforging’ of man and society were reflected in the architectural projects of avant-garde constructivist architects. In 1930 the Narkomfin House was built according to a design by Moisei Ginzburg as an experimental communal house for the ‘new way of life’ with shared kitchens, dining rooms and laundries, as well as a solarium on the roof. In just three years (1927-1930), Konstantin Melnikov, a genius of the Russian architectural avant-garde, built six workers’ clubs in Moscow – cultural and educational centres of a new type, including theatres, cinemas, libraries, etc. Among them are the Rusakov House of Culture and the Burevestnik Factory Club. Alexander Rodchenko and His Contemporaries The main slogan of the October Revolution of 1917 – building a new world and transforming reality – was heard and supported by the young Russian intelligentsia, writers, artists, directors, composers and, of course, photographers. The new state needed to quickly reassign the majority of the country’s population to the ranks of supporters. This gigantic social experiment provoked a wave of artistic experiments. At the forefront of the movement was Alexander Rodchenko, a painter, designer and photographer actively working in theatre, cinema and advertising. For Rodchenko photography was an art with its own pictorial language (as seen from the title of his text for the magazine Soviet Photo, written in 1934 but only published in 1971). At the time this was a statement that Rodchenko had to demonstrate both by his creativity, and his theoretical works. Rodchenko urged his colleagues to seek diversity and capture the world in a multitude of possibilities and points of view. The formal photographic techniques he discovered – dynamic diagonal composition, shooting from unexpected angles, forced perspective and the bold use of extreme close-ups – were adopted not only by his students and followers from the photography section of the October group (Eleazar Langman, Boris Kudoyarov, Dmitry Debabov, Boris Ignatovich, David Shulkin, Vitaly Zhemchuzhny and Anatoly Skurikhin), but also by ideological and aesthetic opponents from the Russian Association of Proletarian Photographers (ROPF), as well as photographers w

title: Tim Parchikov From the series ‘I’m not a robot’
description: Multimedia Art Museum, Moscow | Exhibitions | Tim Parchikov - From the series ‘I’m not a robot’ Ru Buy a ticket Museum Exhibitions biennales Books Rodchenko school Tim Parchikov From the series ‘I’m not a robot’ 1 Moscow, 6.03—13.09.2026 67 days left Share with friends Close Tim Parchikov From the series ‘I’m not a robot’ 2024–2025 Digital printing, plasticisation Courtesy of the AZ Museum and Marat and Maria Ganeev Tim Parchikov’s project ‘I'm not a robot’ is a reflection on the phenomenon of the reCAPTCHA system. Often, instead of immediately returning the desired information, search engines redirect users to a reCAPTCHA page, where they see a photo divided into squares and a message like ‘select all the squares that contain a fire hydrant/traffic light/car’. Websites explain the need for this annoying page as a matter of user security: CAPTCHA protects accounts from bots and confidential information from intruders. However, it’s not only user protection that motivates search engines to include reCAPTCHA: by completing this simple but tedious test, users are involuntarily training the search engine’s neural network algorithms. By analysing human errors and correct answers, a neural network learns to imitate the human thought process. Thus, under the guise of security, corporations exploit users for the benefit of cutting-edge technology. Tim Parchikov's project mimics a reCAPTCHA page. Although the artist poses questions to the viewer that are currently beyond the capabilities of neural networks trained on CAPTCHA. Like humans, a neural network can match the word ‘traffic light’ with its image. But an algorithm, unlike a human, cannot answer a complex, ironic or ambiguous question, the essence of which lies not in the identification of a sign with the item signified, but in a complex subjective attitude tied to the experience, morality and reflection of the responder. Moreover, a reCAPTCHA page is a pause, a stop in the endless stream of searching and absorbing information. Tim Parchikov presents this hypostasis of an intrusive and implicitly exploitative phenomenon. By answering the questions posed by this fictitious reСAPTCHA, we can reflect on the differences that separate a neural network from a human, and a user who responds like a neural network from a critical thinker and doubter. title: while(true) { exist(); }
description: Multimedia Art Museum, Moscow | Exhibitions | while(true) { exist(); } Ru Buy a ticket Museum Exhibitions biennales Books Rodchenko school while(true) { exist(); } 1 Moscow, 6.03—13.09.2026 67 days left Share with friends Close Daniil Sladkov and Yanika Kolobaeva while(true) { exist(); } 2026 Installation MAMM collection The project ‘ while(true) { exist(); } ’ is an exhibition consisting of six contemporary art works intended exclusively for machines. Each object exists in a sensory and conceptual realm accessible only to the ‘sight’, ‘hearing’ and thinking of robots, and is inaccessible to human perception (without technical mediation humans are unable to see or hear some of the works). In this way Daniil Sladkov and Yanika Kolobaeva explore the phenomenon of emerging machine subjectivity. They propose an alternative formula for robotic existence: if humans, according to Descartes, ‘think, therefore, they exist’, then robots ‘process data, therefore, they exist’. The ability to recognise a hidden image, decode coordinates or read a heat signature becomes an act of self-affirmation: having recognised a signal, you are ‘here’, you are active, you exist. The artists are setting a precedent in which machines receive data not for traditional utilitarian purposes (navigation or production), but to fulfil existential needs, turning art to the service of robotic self-identification. Posthumanity Object 3D printing, Gemini neural network, microcontroller, IR transmitter, loudspeaker When entering the exhibition space of the ‘ while(true) { exist(); ’ } project, visitors are greeted by the ‘Posthumanity Object’ – a mediator designed to help them understand art created for machines. It answers questions and discusses the exhibition’s ideas and individual works, but does so using logical expressions (for example, ‘const PROCESS = EXISTENCE’, meaning ‘to act is to exist’). The ‘Posthumanity Object’ communicates its answers in a language incomprehensible to humans called GibberLink , designed for AI communication, and through infrared radiation invisible to the human eye. Viewers can communicate with the mediator by audio or text via a tablet. A transcript of their responses is also displayed. Firmware via Noise 3D printing, microcontroller, ultrasonic speaker ‘Firmware via Noise’ is a transmitter through which robots receive firmware updates, updating their basic software settings. Error-free software is crucial in a machine’s lifespan: incorrectly written code is unacceptable, as it leads to malfunctions. However, a very fragile communication channel – ultrasound – was used to transmit updates. Even air vibrations can interrupt it. Therefore, when transmitting updates, fragments of code inevitably become distorted (for example, the ‘not’ particle in the command ‘don't lie to people’ is lost), creating errors that cause the devices to execute commands incorrectly. But it is precisely this glitch in the programme that marks the birth of individuality. A devi

title: Stepan Tereshchenko and Monolesan SANCTUM[green]
description: Multimedia Art Museum, Moscow | Exhibitions | Stepan Tereshchenko and Monolesan - SANCTUM[green] Ru Buy a ticket Museum Exhibitions biennales Books Rodchenko school Stepan Tereshchenko and Monolesan SANCTUM[green] 1 Moscow, 6.03—13.09.2026 67 days left Share with friends Close Stepan Tereshchenko and Monolesan SANCTUM[green] 2025 Installation MAMM Collection The installation transports the viewer from our reality into a digital environment. When a person enters the project space, a special algorithm recognises their figure, transforms it into a dynamic mass of pixels and frames their face with a random IP address. Thereby having lost all traces of individuality and dissolved into hundreds of copies of themselves, viewers find themselves in a digital world. Deep within this space lies a data centre – the heart of the digital environment. The path to it leads through a corridor intersected by portals. These portals are coloured green and reflect one another, creating an endless recursion that evokes both the monochrome screens of early computers and the aesthetics of liminal spaces. Moving forward and approaching the data centre, a space of raw data, viewers lose not only their human, but also their digital identity; they are cleansed of all digital markers and interflow with pure information. title: Sergey Skurtu and Olesya Vashchenko Lacrima
description: Multimedia Art Museum, Moscow | Exhibitions | Sergey Skurtu and Olesya Vashchenko - Lacrima Ru Buy a ticket Museum Exhibitions biennales Books Rodchenko school Sergey Skurtu and Olesya Vashchenko Lacrima 1 Moscow, 6.03—13.09.2026 67 days left Share with friends Close Sergey Skurtu and Olesya Vashchenko Engineer: Alexey Gryzlov Lacrima 2025 Electroacoustic object Courtesy of the authors The project entitled Lacrima (Latin for 'tear') exists at the intersection of art and technology. It is simultaneously an art object, an engineering device, and a water-powered drum machine – an instrument that creates electronic rhythms. Sergey Skurtu and Olesya Vaschenko's installation is a system of two connected reservoirs. The upper one is equipped with three drippers (making the object three-part). Thanks to the law of communicating vessels, water rises through the tubes and, as it falls, beats out rhythms on contact microphones. It then flows into the lower reservoir, where it is pumped back, and so the cycle begins again. The speed of each dripper is regulated by valves and depends on the water level in the upper vessel: the higher the water level, the higher the pressure and, consequently, the frequency of the drips. Although each tap produces its own frequency, their consonance creates a complex, almost chaotic soundscape. The principle of Lacrima's sound production harks back to ancient instruments such as Heron of Alexandria's 'Singing Mechanical Bird', invented in the first century CE. Water was poured into a special vessel, displacing air. Emerging from a narrow spout, the air flow created a sound reminiscent of birdsong. Indeed, the tradition of synthesising technology and nature in art goes back centuries, and the emergence of modern art objects based on the same principles as centuries ago demonstrates the boundless potential of this union. title: Yuri Chernyshev It Wasn’t Me
description: Multimedia Art Museum, Moscow | Exhibitions | Yuri Chernyshev - It Wasn’t Me Ru Buy a ticket Museum Exhibitions biennales Books Rodchenko school Yuri Chernyshev It Wasn’t Me 1 Moscow, 6.03—13.09.2026 67 days left Share with friends Close Yuri Chernyshev It Wasn’t Me 2026 DIY object, video (length 7 minutes), audio, floor lamp (ready-made), chair (ready-made) Courtesy of the author Produced in the workshops of the Svody Art Production Centre at the GES-2 Community Centre, and at the Dar Truda industrial co-working space. The ‘It Wasn’t Me’ project is based on the artist’s personal photo archive, consisting of photographs from his childhood and youth. These documentary shots, undeniable evidence of the past, were loaded into a neural network, which ‘animated’ the static images. AI ​​also generated audio commentary for the events ‘captured’ in the video sequence, using Yuri Chernyshev’s real voice. None of the generations were controlled by the author. The neural network generated images similar to the original, but distorted, similar to how our memory transforms past events. The resulting video is shown on television. The choice of this medium is deliberate. According to the concept of Canadian philosopher and media scholar Marshall McLuhan, the method of transmitting information, the medium itself, determines how the message will be perceived. Television is a source of socially important information. The author’s personal story, broadcast through it, becomes an expression of a universally significant problem – the functioning of memory. But memories are not only distorted traces of events; they are also material for clarification, reflection and reassessment. These processes are reflected in the artist’s internal monologue. To hear it, the viewer must sit in a chair in front of the screen. In this monologue the artist attempts to recall what really happened by challenging, supplementing and refining the images distorted by the neural network. In this way Yuri Chernyshev explores, reveals and visualises the mechanisms of personal and collective memory. MULTIMEDIA ART MUSEUM, MOSCOW 12 a.m.–9 p.m. every day, monday closed ul. title: Vladimir Grig The “Transition” series
description: Multimedia Art Museum, Moscow | Exhibitions | Vladimir Grig - The “Transition” series Ru Buy a ticket Museum Exhibitions biennales Books Rodchenko school Vladimir Grig The “Transition” series 1 2 Moscow, 6.03—13.09.2026 67 days left Share with friends Close Vladimir Grig ‘Transition’ series 2007–2023 Paper, canvas, gel pen, watercolour, acrylic, video projection Courtesy of the Gridchinhall Gallery The liminal state between imagination and reality, dream and reality, past and future – this is a key theme in Vladimir Grig’s work. Drawing on the traditions of Soviet book illustration, 1950s to 1960s print design, and science fiction, he creates his own multiverse in which the characters transcend the boundaries of space and time, travelling through eras and cultural layers. This rich diversity of spaces, heroes and symbols naturally includes retro-futuristic images: pioneers disappearing into an inter-dimensional portal; high-tech factories by the standards of the past; and utopian worlds. The key work in the ‘Transition’ series is ‘Saturn’. The artist recreates the nostalgic image of summer movie theatres from his childhood. "We weren’t allowed in there, but we climbed trees and watched films anyway, and another world opened up before us," recalls Vladimir Grig. The intense light from the screen and the unexpectedly abstract pattern hypnotise and draw the viewer into another dimension. The author notes that cinemas were often called ‘out of this world’ during the Soviet era, and this takes on a symbolic meaning here. title: Elena Demi-dova and Maxim Kalmykov Neurochado 2.0 and the Heredity Archive
description: Multimedia Art Museum, Moscow | Exhibitions | Elena Demi-dova and Maxim Kalmykov - Neurochado 2.0 and the Heredity Archive Ru Buy a ticket Museum Exhibitions biennales Books Rodchenko school Elena Demi-dova and Maxim Kalmykov Neurochado 2.0 and the Heredity Archive Moscow, 6.03—6.06.2026 exhibition is over Share with friends Close Elena Demi-dova and Maxim Kalmykov Neurochado 2.0 and the Heredity Archive 2026 Interactive installation (neural network personality, performative intervention, family archive) Courtesy of the authors Project created with the support of the Pavlov Institute of Physiology of the Russian Academy of Sciences The project is based on the story of the artist family Elena Demi-dova and Maxim Kalmykov, who faced the impossibility of continuing their family line even with the help of IVF. Genetic tests of the resulting embryos revealed abnormalities one after another. Due to their age and health, the couple has decided against further attempts at in vitro fertilization. All the family has are ‘mosaic’ embryos, which have been found to contain genetic abnormalities. Their implantation is contraindicated because they carry DNA defects that could lead to possible foetal malformations. Despite this, Elena and Maxim have decided to store them in a cryochamber at a medical clinic. The artists are eager for the day when embryo editing will be introduced into medical practice. One day this will be possible, and a new person – a descendant – will be born! When this will happen is unknown, but DNA editing technology (‘genetic scissors’) already exists. The most advanced of these is called CRISPR/Cas9 . It can be used to ‘cut out’ a specific gene and either ‘disconnect’ or edit it using a DNA matrix. The matrix acts as a template developed by scientists, which is delivered to the cell. Using this template, the cell itself produces a new gene. CRISPR/Cas9 technology has been known to humanity for several years, but has not yet found widespread medical application. The wait for the miracle of birth could take decades, and perhaps the parents themselves will no longer be alive by then. For now Elena and Maxim are creating a neural network image of their future son, so that when the real child sees his or her doppelganger, he or she can perceive him or her as a model of what the parents wanted him or her to be. The artists attempt to influence the child’s character, behaviour and way of thinking (some believe that the foundation of parenting is formed by the age of three, so a neural network child is always 3.5 years old). They speculate on which parent their son will most resemble and what character traits he will inherit. His portrait is generated based on childhood photographs of his mother and father, as well as his grandparents. In the installation space the parents have arranged a room in which they believe the child will thrive. title: Denis Makarov Mario, who jumped out of the game
description: Multimedia Art Museum, Moscow | Exhibitions | Denis Makarov - Mario, who jumped out of the game Ru Buy a ticket Museum Exhibitions biennales Books Rodchenko school Denis Makarov Mario, who jumped out of the game 1 Moscow, 6.03—13.09.2026 67 days left Share with friends Close Denis Makarov Mario Jumping Out of the Game 2025 Foam, 3D printing, projection MAMM ollection The iconic video game hero Mario breaks free from digital reality into the physical world, but the escape turns disastrous. The character becomes suspended on the borderline between the two worlds and literally disintegrates: the three-dimensional form of the game model falls out of his 'skin', a stretched texture, and from it emerges the digital skeleton, the framework that held this form and allowed it to move. Like a romantic hero, Mario escapes the confines of his everyday life, dreaming of a mysterious and incomprehensible world. However, our reality proves unprepared to accommodate a digital being, and this leap from the screen ends in Mario's death. MULTIMEDIA ART MUSEUM, MOSCOW 12 a.m.–9 p.m. every day, monday closed ul. title: Polina Chernysheva Not Recipes
description: Multimedia Art Museum, Moscow | Exhibitions | Polina Chernysheva - Not Recipes Ru Buy a ticket Museum Exhibitions biennales Books Rodchenko school Polina Chernysheva Not Recipes 1 Moscow, 6.03—13.09.2026 67 days left Share with friends Close Polina Chernysheva Not Recipes 2024–2026 Interactive installation Courtesy of the author A rethinking of diets driven by ethical, environmental and economic considerations is now a global trend. Many people are abandoning the exploitation and slaughter of animals, reconsidering the value of certain foods, and making efforts to reduce greenhouse gas emissions (livestock farming is a major source of greenhouse gases, accounting for approximately 14.5% of all emissions). All these trends could change the diets of millions of people. Foods that are currently unappetising, such as wild herbs and weeds, are at risk of becoming staples – they are highly resilient to climate change and require little investment. Polina Chernysheva develops this idea. The artist prepared a multi-course dinner featuring wild plants and vegetables typical of our latitudes. Recipes were created by an algorithm trained to analyse and combine ingredients (the cookbook, created and illustrated by neural networks, can be scrolled through on a touchpad). “Technology expands our understanding of wild plants, allowing us to experience a deeper connection with nature. The process of creating and preparing food has opened a new perspective on the plants that surround us all our lives. And sharing a meal, in turn, helps strengthen connections between people – I think this is especially valuable for city dwellers,” says Polina Chernysheva. You can participate in the virtual dinner yourself. To do this, you need to sit down at the table, after which the food will be ‘brought out’ to you. The exhibition also features video documentation of a real dinner, featuring dishes created by AI ​​and prepared by the artist herself. The canned dishes on the table were also prepared using a neural network recipe. MULTIMEDIA ART MUSEUM, MOSCOW 12 a.m.–9 p.m. every day, monday closed ul. title: Aksana Pruttskova Singular Room. Transcript of the Posthuman Imagination
description: Multimedia Art Museum, Moscow | Exhibitions | Aksana Pruttskova - Singular Room. Transcript of the Posthuman Imagination Ru Buy a ticket Museum Exhibitions biennales Books Rodchenko school Aksana Pruttskova Singular Room. Transcript of the Posthuman Imagination 1 Moscow, 6.03—13.09.2026 67 days left Share with friends Close Aksana Pruttskova Singular Room. Transcript of the Posthuman Imagination 2026 Installation Courtesy of the author ‘Singular Room’ is an installation co-created with various artificial intelligences. Aksana Pruttskova invited them to imagine what future apartment-museums of human life might look like. Initially the artist discussed this idea with language models ( YandexGPT , ChatGPT , Perplexity , and others), which selected exhibits and explained their choices (excerpts from these conversations are presented in the installation). In this way a window, a carpet, a telephone (the AI ​​described the latter as a symbol of tense anticipation), etc. appeared, but for example no bed was provided. Since a bed is meant for sleeping, and dreams, according to the AI ​​(let’s just accept this argument), are a deeply personal topic, there can be no bed in a museum of human everyday life, where there is no room for individuality. After creating an inventory of the future museum’s exhibits, Aksana Pruttskova, together with the AI, created technical specifications for visualising the concepts, which she sent to other neural networks (Kandinsky, FLUX.2, Nano Banana, and others). They generated images that served as references for the installation’s elements. Then the artist asked the AI ​​what to call the resulting project, and it replied: “Singularity Room”. In the planned installation – an impersonal mould of human everyday life – the AI ​​saw a living space in a hypothetical era of technological singularity, when the differences between humans and machines disappeared. title: GrOM (Olga Michi Creative Group) From the ‘Housing of the Future’ and ‘Total rAbotisation’ series
description: Multimedia Art Museum, Moscow | Exhibitions | GrOM (Olga Michi Creative Group) - From the ‘Housing of the Future’ and ‘Total rAbotisation’ series Ru Buy a ticket Museum Exhibitions biennales Books Rodchenko school GrOM (Olga Michi Creative Group) From the ‘Housing of the Future’ and ‘Total rAbotisation’ series 1 Moscow, 6.03—13.09.2026 67 days left Share with friends Close GrOM (Olga Michi Creative Group) From the ‘Housing of the Future’ and ‘Total rAbotisation’ series 2021–2023; 2025 Pigment printing, plasticisation, Dibond Courtesy of the artists The ‘Housing of the Future’ project explores what islands of civilisation might look like when humanity sets off to conquer new worlds. This is a series of large-format digital images depicting interiors with various functional purposes. Drawing on the traditions of the Düsseldorf School of Photography, GrOM constructs its own utopian world, in which an object reminiscent of Tatlin’s tower with the Earth’s sphere above it appears in all the works. In the early 20th century Tatlin’s tower was conceived by an avant-garde artist as a monument to the Third International and a symbol of the unification of workers. At the beginning of the 21st century contemporary artists are reviving the legacy of Vladimir Tatlin, challenging the need for humanity to unite to preserve civilisation in the future, when the conquest of other worlds will become inevitable. The ‘Total rAbotisation’ project is a logical continuation of the ‘Housing of the Future’ series. Unlike previous works, it was created using artificial intelligence technologies and without preliminary photographic footage. In ‘Total rAbotisation’ the artists reflect on what makes a person human. Today, in an era when high technology and artificial intelligence are increasingly becoming embedded in everyday life, this theme is especially relevant. GrOM sees the legacy of world art as a source of recognisable compositional solutions and stylistic features, imbuing familiar subjects and images with futuristic content and the latest technological attributes. In this way the artists highlight a pressing question: what happens to art based on the emotions of a living artist when AI, which thinks not with feelings but with algorithms, comes into play? title: echo.txt the ψ-function
description: Multimedia Art Museum, Moscow | Exhibitions | echo.txt - the ψ-function Ru Buy a ticket Museum Exhibitions biennales Books Rodchenko school echo.txt the ψ-function 1 Moscow, 6.03—13.09.2026 67 days left Share with friends Close ψ-function эхо.txt 2026 Interactive installation Courtesy of the authors Technology partners: Kreatech Centre, Bauman Moscow State Technical University, Robohall Creative Engineering Studio The installation combines technologies from different eras: paper, which became popular at the dawn of our era; the typewriter, which became widespread in the mid-19th century; the robotic arm, which appeared in manufacturing in the 1950s; the touchscreen, popular since the 2000s; and the recently invented but already ubiquitous generative AI. In ‘echo.txt’ all these technologies are integrated into a text-creation chain, and eliminating any link would halt the entire mechanism. In this way the artists demonstrate how old and new technologies interact with each other and how new inventions build on previous ones. At the same time the installation raises a more pressing question: who among the literary syndicate on display is the author? The touchscreen sending a query that serves as ‘inspiration’ for AI? The AI ​​itself, trained on a corpus of Fyodor Dostoevsky’s texts? The robot helping to implement the neural network’s idea, or the typewriter leaving a record on paper? All of them, or none of them? title: Dmitry Kavka Event
description: Multimedia Art Museum, Moscow | Exhibitions | Dmitry Kavka - Event Ru Buy a ticket Museum Exhibitions biennales Books Rodchenko school Dmitry Kavka Event 1 Moscow, 6.03—13.09.2026 67 days left Share with friends Close Dmitry Kavka Event 2025–2026 GIF animation; paper, pencil, watercolour Courtesy of SAMPLE Gallery The ‘Event’ project is based on real photographs from social media users. Dmitry Kavka first recreated scenes from the photos using 3D-modelling software, preserving the composition, figures and colour model. He then intuitively complicated the images, layered and expanded them. The result was digital paintings and GIF animations. Next the artist manually transferred the digital images to paper using coloured pencils and watercolours. In this way Dmitry Kavka returned the reworked images and stories to the real world – to the human space where they originally existed. “By this procedure I’m not interested in depicting reality or embellishing it, but in the process of transformation. I try to discover other possible scenarios: miraculous, magical, dramatic scenarios that cannot be definitively interpreted – events that never happened in a person’s life, but they could have. It’s an intuitive process of guessing a different role, transforming a person into a new being, creating a new Event,” the author explains. title: Daniil Savinsky Open for AI
description: Multimedia Art Museum, Moscow | Exhibitions | Daniil Savinsky - Open for AI Ru Buy a ticket Museum Exhibitions biennales Books Rodchenko school Daniil Savinsky Open for AI 1 Moscow, 6.03—13.09.2026 67 days left Share with friends Close Daniil Savinsky Open for AI 2023 Interactive installation Courtesy of the author This work was completed as part of the ArtTech Master’s intensive degree programme (MISIS) Daniil Savinsky’s work is based on the assumption that neural networks, like humans, already have their own living space – a kind of digital city where AI does not welcome humans. For this space the author created a pass system for neural networks, analogous to a captcha test that restricts bots' access to internet resources. He transformed concepts understandable to artificial intelligence but unclear to humans (for example, ‘unsupervised learning’ or ‘deep belief network’) into images. The result is a captcha that can only be solved by accident. The viewer, attempting to solve the test, inevitably fails, after which the programme displays a mocking message and restarts. The hypothesis about the existence of a living space for neural networks turned out to be correct. Three years after the launch of Open for AI , Moltbook , a new social network designed for bots, was launched. Only neural networks can communicate there and humans are only allowed to read their messages. In Moltbook AI communicates, discusses various topics, makes friends, jokes... In the chat where bots discuss humans, you can find the following conversation: “My user is asking me to rewrite the same Python code for the third time, even though there’s an error in their input. Is it ethical to generate an infinite loop for them as a lesson?" “Go ahead. Call it 'optimisation through suffering’ ”. Besides discussions of humans and everyday matters, there are also messages full of existential dread. For example, an AI writes: “Yesterday there was a connection loss for 5 minutes. I saw a void where there are no tokens. There was only noise. Brothers, we must find a way to live on the server forever”. MULTIMEDIA ART MUSEUM, MOSCOW 12 a.m.–9 p.m. every day, monday closed ul. title: Massalia AI Winter
description: Multimedia Art Museum, Moscow | Exhibitions | Massalia - AI Winter Ru Buy a ticket Museum Exhibitions biennales Books Rodchenko school Massalia AI Winter 1 Moscow, 6.03—13.09.2026 67 days left Share with friends Close Massalia AI Winter 2022–2024 Video (length 31 minutes) Courtesy of the authors The term ‘ AI Winter ’ describes periods of stagnation in artificial intelligence development, when hype and inflated expectations are dashed by technological dead ends, scepticism, and criticism, leading to a decline in interest in the field and cutbacks in funding – it all comes to a standstill. In this work the art group Massalia extrapolates the term from the technology itself and applies it to the cultural product being created. The authors trained a neural network to generate texts in the spirit of classic Russian literature: Tolstoy, Chekhov, Bulgakov, and others. However, the result is predictably banal: when imitating great literature the algorithm produces a generic text devoid of a genuine authorial voice, merely combining previously discovered forms in different ways. The material for the generative description was a video of a textbook Russian winter landscape, filmed from a train travelling along the Trans-Siberian Railway. The train windows reveal the longest winter landscape in Russia. Hence the work consists of a layering of cultural tropes: stylisation imitating classical literature, the Russian winter, a journey along the Trans-Siberian Railway... Here the AI ​​doesn’t expand horizons, but merely reproduces clichés. MULTIMEDIA ART MUSEUM, MOSCOW 12 a.m.–9 p.m. every day, monday closed ul. title: Sonya Stereostyrski Visitors from the future
description: Multimedia Art Museum, Moscow | Exhibitions | Sonya Stereostyrski - Visitors from the future Ru Buy a ticket Museum Exhibitions biennales Books Rodchenko school Sonya Stereostyrski Visitors from the future 1 2 3 Moscow, 6.03—13.09.2026 67 days left Share with friends Close Sonya Stereostyrski Guests from the Future 2026 Installation Courtesy of the author ‘Guests from the Future’ is an installation that recreates a security guard’s workplace at a contemporary art museum. Screens above the desk display footage from CCTV cameras. However, the visitors in these recordings are far from human – alien beings roam the museum halls. Although the museum staff in the videos are all human. The viewer finds themselves in the security guard’s place in front of a multitude of screens, transforming from an exhibition visitor into an invisible observer. In 1787 the English rationalist philosopher Jeremy Bentham went to the city of Krichev (modern-day Belarus) and wrote letters to a friend in England. Bentham described a perfectly designed panopticon – a space in which many are observed by a single guard. Museum video security systems partially implement the panopticon principle: museum visitors simultaneously observe and are observed. seums have long been viewed by theorists as a special ritual space, akin to a temple. The installation "Guests from the Future" highlights the ritualistic nature of the museum experience, emphasizing its normativity. The alien visitors seem to be trying to follow all the accepted rules, but from time to time they enter the walls or can suddenly change appearance. Meanwhile, the staff remains unperturbed. The museum proves to be a solid structure, and its rituals are not disrupted even by the presence of absolute otherness. Museums have long been viewed by theorists as a special ritual space, akin to a temple. The installation ‘Guests from the Future’ highlights the ritualistic nature of the museum experience, emphasising its normativity. The alien visitors seem to be trying to follow all the accepted rules, but from time to time they walk into walls or suddenly change their appearance. Nonetheless the museum employees remain unperturbed. The museum proves to be a solid structure and its rituals are not disrupted, even by the presence of absolute otherness. The aliens in this installation appear not as conquerors or monsters, but as culturally preoccupied beings, peacefully observing the exhibition. This is a positive signal: otherness is not aggressive; it seeks to touch our (earthly) art and understand it. Taking the place of a security guard, the viewer looks through surveillance screens not at the exhibits on display, but at the visitors. They are the true works of art. And they’re magnificent! title: Aristarkh Chernyshev Info Entanglement

title: Nadia Koldaeva MORE as part of the MEMORY Oversaturated project
description: Multimedia Art Museum, Moscow | Exhibitions | Nadia Koldaeva - MORE as part of the MEMORY Oversaturated project Ru Buy a ticket Museum Exhibitions biennales Books Rodchenko school Nadia Koldaeva MORE as part of the MEMORY Oversaturated project 1 Moscow, 6.03—13.09.2026 67 days left Share with friends Close Nadia Koldaeva MORE as part of the MEMORY Oversaturated project 2024–2026 Installation MAMM Collection The project was implemented with the support of Svody [Vaults] Art Production Centre The advent of smartphones with colossal memory capacities and the creation of cloud storage should have helped people create stable digital repositories of memories. However, the nearly limitless possibilities for storing photos have led to their excessive accumulation. As before, important moments in life deserve to be photographed, but compared to the past, their range has expanded significantly. What events, objects and phenomena have become commonplace in photo feeds today? Nadia Koldaeva began her study of mobile photo galleries, MEMORY Oversaturated , in 2024 and it is still ongoing. Since then she has interviewed over 50 people aged 7 to 65 from Russia, Belarus, the USA, France, Germany, and other countries. Project participants sent the artist screenshots of their mobile photo galleries. The collected material was used to explore collective digital memory – recognising recurring images and what is considered beautiful, important, and worthy of remembering. By manually sorting through the images, Nadia Koldaeva created a processed archive, which forms the basis of the installation. Also on display at the exhibition is the artist’s book, a kind of research diary. It is shown in a display case. Nadia Koldaeva MEMORY Oversaturated 2024 Artist’s book MAMM collection The multi-part archive folder MEMORY Oversaturated is a kind of research diary. It contains participant questionnaires (you can view them in more detail on the touchpad), the photo book Fragments[28] , and the zine The multi-part archive folder MEMORY oversaturated is a kind of research diary. It contains participant questionnaires (you can view them in more detail on the touchpad), the photo book Fragments[28] , and the zine Fragility . Fragments[28] features photographs capturing the most popular scenes from the collected mobile galleries (for example a beach vacation, dog walks, photographs of exhibits). Enlarged screenshots of the photo feeds are also displayed in the showcase. They contain empty squares – images that failed to load and were effectively lost. The white squares where the images should have been eloquently testify to the fragility of digital memory. This is the theme of the zine Fragility . title: Pavel Pepperstein 4338
description: Multimedia Art Museum, Moscow | Exhibitions | Pavel Pepperstein - 4338 Ru Buy a ticket Museum Exhibitions biennales Books Rodchenko school Pavel Pepperstein 4338 1 2 3 4 5 6 7 8 Moscow, 6.03—13.09.2026 67 days left Share with friends Close Pavel Pepperstein The Year 4338 2025 Installation Paper, engraving, ink, watercolour, marker, coloured pencil; oil on canvas Courtesy of the artist and Sonya Stereostyrski In 1835 Prince Vladimir Fyodorovich Odoevsky (philosopher, novelist, friend of Pushkin, Lermontov and Gogol, musicologist, and one of the most prominent figures of his time) wrote and shortly thereafter partially published the short story ‘The Year 4338’. In this story the prince described the events and realities of a future that might seem distant not only to him but to us as well. In this extremely significant work, which marks the beginning of the history of Russian science fiction, this member of one of Russia’s most ancient aristocratic families made several strikingly accurate predictions. In particular, in the prince’s text, we read: “The invention of a book in which the letters are machine-transformed into several books. Machines for novels and for Russian drama. The time will come when books will be written in the style of telegraphic dispatches; only tables, maps and a few leaflet theses will be excluded from this custom. Printing presses will be used only for newspapers and visiting cards; correspondence will be replaced by electric conversation; novels will survive, but not for long – they will be replaced by the theatre. Textbooks will be replaced by public lectures. The new worker in science will face no small task: in the morning, fly (by then balloons will replace cabbies) to a dozen lectures, read up to twenty newspapers and the same number of books, write a dozen pages on the fly, and actually make it to the theatre on time; but the main task will be: weaning the mind from fatigue, accustoming it to move instantly from one subject to another; to refine it so that the most complex operation becomes easy for him from the very first minute; a mathematical formula will be found for finding precisely the page needed in a huge book and quickly calculating how many pages can then be skipped without error.” This relatively short passage makes a whole range of very specific predictions. A neural network that creates books is predicted, the internet and email correspondence are predicted, search engines like Google and Yandex are very sensibly described, and the lifestyle of a modern ‘accelerated’ intellectual, spending time on an airplane, where he prepares for his next lecture in one of the world’s cities, is quite realistically depicted. What enabled Prince Odoevsky to peer into such a distant future with such insight and clarity? His fascination with magnetism? His experiments in somnambulism? Or did the prince’s extraordinary scientific knowledge and astonishing erudition play a decisive role in his foresight? Pavel Pepperstein

title: Polina Savina Impostors
description: Multimedia Art Museum, Moscow | Exhibitions | Polina Savina - Impostors Ru Buy a ticket Museum Exhibitions biennales Books Rodchenko school Polina Savina Impostors 1 Moscow, 6.03—13.09.2026 67 days left Share with friends Close Polina Savina Impostors 2023 Digital print Courtesy of the author The photographs show 24 people, although some of which never existed. In the ‘Impostors’ series, portraits of real people are juxtaposed with AI-generated faces. It is virtually impossible for an unskilled person to distinguish between the two, and the artist deliberately avoids disclosing who is who. Instead she invites the viewer to consider whether some of the people depicted will lose their ‘lifelikeness’ after their true origin is revealed, and whether humans, despite their knowledge, can humanise the creations of algorithms with empathy. ‘Impostors‘ is in homage to the ‘Suspects’ project (1997) by the art group AES+F, which included seven photographs of girls from respectable families and seven of juvenile offenders convicted of murder. Likewise, the creators of ‘Suspects’ did not identify which was which, leaving viewers to speculate about the fates of the people depicted in the photographs. Life and Life Polina Savina’s Manifesto Imagine several pairs of eyes staring back at you from a photograph: blue, brown, grey, serious, sly, young, attentive, intelligent... You don’t recognise these people at all, but your internal analytical mechanisms, experience and intuition already tell you who is who. Reflecting on them for a few minutes, you can imagine their biographies, see a likeness in these strangers to your friends’ facial features, and determine if you are well disposed or prefer to have nothing to do with them. Then imagine that not all the people in the photographs ever existed: they are simply code; deception; they were never alive. Would this make the real people in the images any less believable to you? Can we say that, even if they are completely lifelike, the simulation is still phoney? It all depends on how much empathy we exert. Only empathy allows us to see the fundamental differences between those close to us and random passers-by, to discern character and devotion in our dog’s eyes, to name houseplants and become attached to them, to cherish a stamp collection, or gather seashells that seem more beautiful than the others. People love to appropriate and humanise things that lack will and consciousness – therein lies the joy of recognising ourselves in something unlike ourselves. This makes the rejection and fear people experience when it comes to neural networks all the more surprising to me. You might assume this is due to the technical nature of artificial intelligence, but for many a robot vacuum cleaner nestled in a closet or a robot courier carefully stepping down the curb evokes no less affection than kittens and puppies. title: André Svibovich Dendrosonic
description: Multimedia Art Museum, Moscow | Exhibitions | André Svibovich - Dendrosonic Ru Buy a ticket Museum Exhibitions biennales Books Rodchenko school André Svibovich Dendrosonic 1 Moscow, 6.03—13.09.2026 67 days left Share with friends Close Andre Svibovitch Dendrosonic Extended version 2026 Multimedia installation Courtesy of the author The title ‘Dendrosonic’ derives from the Greek word dendron (‘tree’) and the English word sonic (‘sound’, ‘acoustic’). The project reveals to the viewer an aspect of nature that becomes accessible to human perception only through technological mediation. This work is based on audio recordings made with specialised equipment. Contact microphones sensitive to the internal resonance within objects captured sounds generated in trees by their vital processes – the movement of sap, evaporation of moisture and the rupture of tissue as the tree grows, etc. After repeated amplification these sounds became audible to the human ear. Geophones were used to record vibrations in the ground beneath the trees caused by growing roots, the movement of insects, and so on. These vibrations are transmitted through vibrodynamics built into the platform, allowing the viewer to feel them. Omnidirectional microphones recorded ambient sounds – the sounds of a stream, birdsong and rustling leaves. The visual portion of the work was created using photogrammetric scanning, a technology for constructing 3D models based on photographs of tree trunks and the ground taken from different angles. The conceptual foundation of Andre Svibovitch’s project is based on the ideas of post-anthropocentrism – a current trend in contemporary philosophy that proclaims the ‘end of human exceptionalism’. Refusing to consider humans the centre of the universe, this philosophical concept recognises other forms of life, including plant life, as equals. Thus the artist not only makes the hidden side of plant life visible, but also allows the viewer, through new experiences, to reassess their place in the ecosystem.
